entry.title: Re: Cross-Plant PPDS Planning: Special Procurement Not Tr...
entry.content:
Step 1: Special Procurement Key
In Plant A, assign Special Procurement 97 (procure from Plant B) in the material master (MRP 2 view). Confirm that procurement type is F (external procurement). This tells MRP that supply should come from Plant B.

Step 2: Source List
In Plant A, create a source list entry for Material 123 with supplying plant = Plant B. Mark it as fixed source if you want PP/DS to always choose Plant B. Without a source list, PP/DS may not recognize Plant B as a valid source.

Step 3: Transportation Lane
In PP/DS, check that a transportation lane exists from Plant B → Plant A. Use the “Create from Special Procurement” function to generate it automatically. Ensure validity dates cover your planning horizon.

Step 4: PDS in Supplying Plant
In Plant B, make sure Material 123 has Procurement type E (in‑house production), A valid BOM and routing/master recipe and a PP/DS PDS generated (via CIF integration or in‑system generation). This allows PP/DS to create planned orders in Plant B.

Step 5: MRP Live Execution
Run MD01N for Plant A. Expected outcome is Stock Transfer Requisition (or STR → PR) created in Plant A. Requirement transferred to Plant B. PP/DS generates a planned order in Plant B.

Step 6: Validation
Check MD04 in Plant A → STR should appear. Check /SAPAPO/RRP3 or PP/DS product view in Plant B → planned order should appear. If not, review logs for missing source determination or invalid transportation lane.

Step 7: Common Pitfalls

  • Source list missing or not valid.
  • Transportation lane dates not valid.
  • PDS not generated in Plant B.
  • Special procurement maintained incorrectly (wrong key or missing).
  • MRP Live restrictions (some classic MRP behaviors differ).

Recommendation:
Follow this checklist in order. The most common root cause in your scenario is missing source list in Plant A or invalid transportation lane. Once those are corrected, MRP Live should generate the STR in Plant A and the planned order in Plant B.

entry.title: How to Get Started with SAP Sustainability Data Exchange
entry.content:
Why SAP Sustainability Data Exchange Matters

Sustainability doesn’t stop at company boundaries — it extends across the entire supply chain. To truly reduce environmental impact, organizations must collaborate and exchange reliable data with their partners.

SAP Sustainability Data Exchange enables businesses to securely share product carbon footprint data, improving transparency and accelerating decarbonization efforts.

With SAP Sustainability Data Exchange, companies can:

  • Collaborate with partners on sustainability data
  • Increase transparency across the supply chain
  • Standardize and share product carbon footprint information
  • Support data-driven decarbonization initiatives

What You’ll Learn in This Learning Journey

This short, 1-hour 22-minute course introduces the key concepts and practical steps to start using SAP Sustainability Data Exchange:

  1. Introduction and Business Challenges
    Understand supply chain sustainability challenges and how SAP Sustainability Data Exchange enables collaboration between business partners.
  2. Leveraging Networks
    Learn how the solution integrates with networks like:
  • SAP Business Network
  • Catena-X
  1. Subscription and Setup
    Learn how to provision and adapt  SAP Sustainability Data Exchange within your SAP Business Technology Platform environment.
  2. Getting Started with Data Exchange

      Learn how to:

  • Maintain master data for products and business partners
  • Exchange product carbon footprint data
  • Understand end-to-end carbon management scenarios with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management

Who Should Take the Course?

This learning journey is ideal for:

  • Consultants supporting sustainability and SAP implementations

No prerequisites are required, making it a great starting point for beginners.

Start collaborating on supply chain sustainability today and explore the Learning Journey “Getting Started with SAP Sustainability Data Exchange” here.

entry.title: SII Tip for Spain #10: Error 1166 in Canary Islands and Regime 15
entry.content:
In SII Tip #7, we covered Error 1166 ("El valor del campo TipoImpositivo no está incluido en la lista de valores permitidos") . In this follow-up post, we dive into a very specific — and commonly misunderstood — scenario that triggers the same error: incoming invoices from retailer companies in the Canary Islands under Régimen 15.

 

Background: The Canary Islands Fiscal Regime

The Canary Islands, while part of Spain and the European Union, operate under a distinct economic and fiscal framework. Due to their geographical location as an outermost region of the EU, the scarcity of natural resources, and their distance from the European continent, the islands have their own indirect taxation system.

The key difference: Value Added Tax (VAT) does not apply in the Canary Islands. Instead, supplies of goods and services are subject to the General Indirect Canary Tax (IGIC — Impuesto General Indirecto Canario).

The valid IGIC tax rates are defined in the article 51 of the Law 4/2012. In 2026, the applicable rates are:

 

IGIC RateDescription
0%Zero rate (tipo cero)
3%Reduced rate (tipo reducido)
5%Reduced rate
7%General rate (tipo general)
9.5%Increased rate
15%Special increased rate
20%Special rate (tobacco)

When the Canary Islands Tax Agency validates SII submissions, it checks that the <TipoImpositivo> field contains one of these accepted values. Any other value triggers Error 1166.

 

Incoming invoices from retailers in the Canary Islands — Regime 15

Under Canary Islands tax law, purchases from retailers (comerciantes minoristas) are exempted according to article 27 of the Real Decreto 1/2025 of the Canary Islands, however the buyer can deduct the implicit tax amount for the corresponding transactions, provided that the supplier states in the invoice the status of retailer company (“Comerciante Minorista”).

 

What is the Implicit Tax Charge (Carga Impositiva Implícita)?

The calculation of the implicit tax amount is defined in the.Article 29.3 of the IGIC Law (Ley 20/1991)

When a company purchases goods from a retailer company, the buyer can deduct an implicit tax charge (carga impositiva implícita) calculated using a coefficient K:

The implicit tax charge amount is then:

In the SII incoming invoices book (Libro de Facturas Recibidas), retailer invoices under Regime 15 have specific requirements:

  • The invoice must be classified with Clave de Régimen = "15" in the <ClaveRegimenEspecialOTrascendencia> field of the received invoices XML.
  • Instead of the standard <CuotaSoportada> (tax amount), the XML must use <CargaImpositivaImplicita> (Implicit Tax Charge).
  • The <TipoImpositivo> field must report the actual IGIC rate, not the coefficient.

 

The Coefficient (K) — How SAP Calculates the IGIC Rate

In SAP's SII solution, the system expects tax codes to be configured with the coefficient (K), not the actual IGIC rate. SAP then back-calculates the real IGIC rate using the inverse formula: IGIC Rate = Coefficient (K) / 0.70

This calculated IGIC rate is what SAP sends to the Tax Agency in the <TipoImpositivo> field.

 

The Problem

The error 1166: "El valor del campo TipoImpositivo no está incluido en la lista de valores permitidos" ("The value of the TipoImpositivo field is not included in the list of allowed values") occurs when customers configure the tax code with the actual IGIC rate instead of the coefficient (K).
 
For example:  suppose a customer creates a tax code for a retailer company supplier with the standard IGIC rate of 7%:
  1. Tax code is configured with rate: 7% (incorrect — this should be the coefficient 4.9%)
  2. SAP's SII process applies the back-calculation: 7% / 0.70 = 10%
  3. SAP sends <TipoImpositivo>10.00</TipoImpositivo> to the Tax Agency
  4. The Canary Islands Tax Agency rejects the submission: 10% is not a valid IGIC rate
  5. ❌ Error 1166 is returned

Root Cause: The tax code percentage was set to the IGIC rate (7%) instead of the coefficient K (4.9%). SAP's conversion formula produces an invalid rate (10%), which is rejected by the Tax Agency.

The Solution

Configure your tax codes for retailer company suppliers (Régimen 15) using the coefficient (K), not the IGIC tax rate. For example:

 

This means that you need to identify which tax codes will be used for incoming invoices from retailer company suppliers in the Canary Islands and use  the coefficient (K) in those tax codes

  • For IGIC 3% → set the tax code rate to 2.1%
  • For IGIC 5% → set the tax code rate to 3.5%
  • For IGIC 7% → set the tax code rate to 4.9%
  • ...

When properly configured with coefficient K = 4.9% for a 7% IGIC operation, the SII XML for a received invoice under Régimen 15 should look like this:

<FacturaRecibida>
  <TipoFactura>F1</TipoFactura>
  <ClaveRegimenEspecialOTrascendencia>15</ClaveRegimenEspecialOTrascendencia>
  <DesgloseFactura>
    <DesgloseIGIC>
      <DetalleIGIC>
        <TipoImpositivo>7.00</TipoImpositivo>
        <BaseImponible>1000.00</BaseImponible>
        <CargaImpositivaImplicita>49.00</CargaImpositivaImplicita>
      </DetalleIGIC>
    </DesgloseIGIC>
  </DesgloseFactura>
</FacturaRecibida>

Notice:

  • <ClaveRegimenEspecialOTrascendencia> = 15 (retailer company)
  • <TipoImpositivo> = 7.00 (the real IGIC rate, correctly derived from K)
  • <CargaImpositivaImplicita> is used instead of <CuotaSoportada>
  • Implicit tax charge: 1000 × 4.9% = 49.00

 

Further Reading

In case of additional questions, you can visit the SII help documentation in the following links:

  • 📘 Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • 📘 Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Private Cloud
  • 📘 Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in ERP
  • 📝 SII Tip for Spain #7: Error 1166 and Tax Procedures
  • SAP Note 2753243 - Canary Island: How to convert "Implicit IGIC" included in prices into some posted tax line item

 

Disclaimer

This blog post reflects the author's interpretation of Spanish legal requirements and SAP system behavior as of the publication date. It is intended for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. For specific guidance, consult your legal or tax advisor and refer to official SAP documentation.

The links to external sources in this blog may be modified at any time externally by the owner without previous notice.

entry.title: Modern UI Email Weather Alerts with SAP Integration Suite — Automated Threshold‑Based Notification
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

The business conditions are very dependent on the current weather situation. In industries like logistics, transportation, field service and others, changes in temperature or even harsh weather conditions will affect the daily operation and employee security. In order to prevent this, there is a need for a automated and reliable solution to monitor the conditions and receive a notification on scheduled basis, when certain levels are crossed (e.g. Extreme heat, cold, freezing conditions etc.).

BUSINESS SCENARIO

The purpose of this blog is to show how SAP Integration Suite (CPI) can be used to automate weather tracking and send alerts based on predefined temperature thresholds. The iFlow evaluates current weather data, identifies unusual temperature conditions, and triggers polished, user‑friendly email notifications.
WHAT THE IFLOW DOES
  • Automatically triggers on a configured schedule using a Timer Start Event.
  • Fetches real-time weather data from OpenWeather for a configurable city.
  • Classifies temperature using a Groovy-based logic with the following categories:
    • Extreme Hot → when temperature is 40°C or above
    • Hot → when temperature is between 35°C and 39°C
    • Warm → when temperature is between 26°C and 34°C
    • Normal → when temperature is between 18°C and 25°C
    • Cool → when temperature is between 10°C and 17°C
    • Cold → when temperature is between 1°C and 9°C
    • Freezing → when temperature is 0°C or below
    • Unknown → when no valid temperature value is received
  • Determines routing behavior:
    • If Normal → No email is sent.
    • If Abnormal → A polished HTML email alert is generated and sent via the Mail Adapter.
  • Uses secure email authentication, such as a Gmail App Password stored in CPI Security Material.

IMPLEMENTATION FLOW OVERVIEW

This diagram presents a high‑level overview of the complete implementation flow for creating the Scheduled Weather Alert iFlow in SAP Integration Suite. It highlights each major phase—from initial setup (API key configuration and email credential creation) to package setup, and the key iFlow components such as the Timer event, Content Modifier, HTTP integration, Groovy processing, Router logic, and Mail adapter. It also illustrates the final stages of deployment and monitoring within the Integration Suite.

PREREQUISITES

  • An active SAP BTP trial account with Integration Suite subscription and corresponding roles assigned to it.   
Note: For guidance on creating a SAP BTP trial account, refer to Get an Account on SAP BTP Trial To activate and subscribe to the Integration Suite service, see Set Up Integration Suite Trial
  • OpenWeather API key (Free/paid; One Call or Current Weather endpoint).OpenWeather API ) 
  • Gmail App Password (if using Gmail SMTP). 
Steps to Create an OpenWeather API
  • Open the Website
    • Go to: OpenWeather
  • Create a Free Account
    • Click Sign Up (top-right)
    • Enter your email, password
    • Verify your email via the link sent to your inbox
    • After verification, log in using your credentials
  • Choose Weather API
    • Use the Current Weather Data API
https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather?q={city name},{country code}&appid={API key} 
  • Go to “My API Keys”
    • Click your profile icon
    • Select My API Keys

 

You will see a default API key
Copy The API Key for later use
 
Steps to create Mail Credentials — Gmail App Password & Security Material 
  •  Generate Gmail App Password
    • Enable 2-Step Verification in your Google Account
    • Search For → App passwords. 
    • Create a new app password (e.g., Mail)
    • Copy the 16-character App Password (keep it safe).
  •  Add CPI Security Material 
    • Go to your Integration Suite
    • Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Manage Security Material → Create → User Credentials

    • Type: User Credentials 
    • Name: GmailCredential
    • User: your Gmail address (the same email used to generate the App Password) 
    • Password: the App Password generated earlier.

 

  • Connectivity Test (SMTP – Gmail)  
    • Go to your Integration Suite
    • Go to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Connectivity Test

    • Choose SMTP as the protocol 
    • Fill in the following details: 
      • Host: smtp.gmail.com 
      • Port: 587
      • Authentication: Plain User/Password 
      • Credential Name: Select the Gmail User Credential created earlier in Security Material 
      • Click Send  
        • If successful → Your SMTP setup is correct 
        • If error → Review the error message and reconfigure the settings accordingly 

DESIGNING THE IFLOW

STEPS 1: Create an Integration Package 
  • Open SAP Integration Suite → Design → Integrations & APIs. 
  • Click Create
  • Fill Package Details
    • Name: Weather Forecast Alert 
    • Short Description: Automated Weather Alert System using OpenWeather API and Email Notification.
    • Save the package
STEPS 2: Create a New Integration Flow 
  • Add Artifact - Inside your package:  
  • Click Add → Integration Flow.
  • Fill Iflow Details
    • Name: IFL_WeatherAlert 
    • Description:
      • Interface Type - Synchronous 
      • Overall Flow - Timer → SAP CPI → OpenWeather API → Mail 
      • Receiver Method - GET 
    • Click Add and Open in Editor
STEPS 3: Timer Event 
To schedule the iFlow so it automatically triggers at fixed intervals or at a specific time, ensuring weather data is fetched and processed without manual execution.
  • Drag Start Timer from palette. 

  • Scheduler → Configuration: 
  • Default Value: Choose Basic
  • Frequency: 
    • Enter As: Simple Schedule
    • Repeat: Repeat Daily 
    • Every: 1 day
  • Time Range:
    • Start Date & Time: set required start (e.g., 2026‑03‑13 11:22)
    • End Date & Time: optional
  • Time Zone: Use UTC +5:30 — India Standard Time (Asia/Kolkata) or choose the time zone required for your business scenario
  • Throw Exception on Schedule Expiry: Enable if needed ✔  

STEPS 4: Content Modifier — Set Parameters

To prepare and organize message data by setting required properties, headers, or body content. 

  • Drag Content Modifier into flow. 
  • Go to the Exchange Property Tab.
  • Add Properties: 
    • Name: cityName
      Source Type: Constant
      Source Value: Hyderabad
    • Name: countryCode
      Source Type: Constant
      Source Value: IN
    • Name: appid
      Source Type: Constant
      Source Value: Your OpenWeather API Key 
STEPS 5: Request‑Reply 
The Request‑Reply step is used when an iFlow must call an external API and wait for its response before moving forward. In this use case, SAP CPI sends a request to the OpenWeather API, receives the weather data in real time, and then uses that response to format the output and trigger the email notification.
  • Drag Request‑Reply into the flow (place it after the Content Modifier where you build your API request).
STEPS 5: Receiver
  • Drag a Receiver from the palette onto the iFlow. 
  • Rename it to OpenWeatherConnect 
    (This name will help you identify that it is used for the weather API call.) 
  • Draw a connector from the Request‑Reply step to OpenWeatherConnect. 
  • When prompted for adapter selection, choose HTTP Adapter.

Configure HTTP Adapter (Receiver → OpenWeatherConnect) 

  • Address: Provide the OpenWeather API Base URL 
    https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather 
  • Query Parameters: Will be added inside Content Modifier or Request‑Reply earlie
q=${property.cityName},${property.countryCode}&appid=${property.appid} 
  • Method: GET 
  • Authentication: None 
  • Request Headers: *  

STEPS 6: Groovy Script — Classify & Extract

Parse the OpenWeather JSON response, extract important fields (city, country, temp, temp_min, temp_max, humidity, wind speed, timestamp), classify the temperature into COLD / WARM / HOT, and set the required properties for the next steps such as routing and email formatting.  

  • Drag a Groovy Script right after the Request‑Reply. 
  • Add New Script → Click + icon to add 
  • Paste the script below.
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import java.time.format.DateTimeFormatter
import java.math.RoundingMode
import java.time.*
import java.io.Reader

Message processData(Message message) {

    def bodyObj = message.getBody(java.io.Reader) 
    def json = new JsonSlurper().parse(bodyObj)
    
    
    def toC = { BigDecimal v ->
        if (v == null) return null
        BigDecimal c = (v > 200) ? (v - 273.15G) : v
        c.setScale(1, RoundingMode.HALF_UP)
    }

    def toCardinal = { Integer deg ->
        if (deg == null) return "N/A"
        String[] dirs = ["N","NNE","NE","ENE","E","ESE","SE","SSE","S","SSW","SW","WSW","W","WNW","NW","NNW"]
        int idx = Math.floor(((deg % 360) / 22.5) + 0.5) as int
        dirs[idx % 16]
    }

    def classify = { BigDecimal c ->
        if (c == null) return "Unknown"
        if (c >= 40)       "Extreme Heat"
        else if (c >= 35)  "Hot"
        else if (c >= 26)  "Warm"
        else if (c >= 18)  "Normal"
        else if (c >= 10)  "Cool"
        else if (c >= 1)   "Cold"
        else               "Freezing"
    }

    def fmtLocalDateTime = { Long epochSec, ZoneOffset z, DateTimeFormatter fmt ->
        try { (epochSec ? ZonedDateTime.ofInstant(Instant.ofEpochSecond(epochSec), z).format(fmt) : "N/A") }
        catch (Exception e) { "N/A" }
    }
    def fmtLocalTime = { Long epochSec, ZoneOffset z, DateTimeFormatter fmt ->
        try { (epochSec ? ZonedDateTime.ofInstant(Instant.ofEpochSecond(epochSec), z).format(fmt) : "N/A") }
        catch (Exception e) { "N/A" }
    }
    def fmtLocalDate = { Long epochSec, ZoneOffset z, DateTimeFormatter fmt ->
        try { (epochSec ? ZonedDateTime.ofInstant(Instant.ofEpochSecond(epochSec), z).format(fmt) : "N/A") }
        catch (Exception e) { "N/A" }
    }

    def categoryColor = { String cat ->
        switch (cat) {
            case "Extreme Heat": return "#b91c1c"
            case "Hot":          return "#ef4444"
            case "Warm":         return "#f59e0b"
            case "Normal":       return "#10b981"
            case "Cool":         return "#3b82f6"
            case "Cold":         return "#2563eb"
            case "Freezing":     return "#0ea5e9"
            default:             return "#111827"

        }
    }

    // -------- 3) Extract fields --------
    String city        = (json?.name ?: "Unknown") as String
    String country     = (json?.sys?.country ?: "") as String
    String loc         = country ? "${city}, ${country}" : city
    String condition   = (json?.weather && json.weather.size() > 0) ? (json.weather[0]?.main ?: "N/A") : "N/A"
    String description = (json?.weather && json.weather.size() > 0) ? (json.weather[0]?.description ?: "N/A") : "N/A"

    BigDecimal tempRaw   = (json?.main?.temp       ?: 0) as BigDecimal
    BigDecimal feelsRaw  = (json?.main?.feels_like ?: 0) as BigDecimal
    Integer humidity     = (json?.main?.humidity   ?: 0) as Integer
    Integer pressure     = (json?.main?.pressure   ?: 0) as Integer
    Integer visibilityM  = (json?.visibility       ?: 0) as Integer
    BigDecimal windSpeed = (json?.wind?.speed      ?: 0) as BigDecimal
    Integer windDeg      = (json?.wind?.deg        ?: 0) as Integer
    Integer cloudCover   = (json?.clouds?.all      ?: 0) as Integer

    Long fetchTs   = (json?.dt ?: 0L) as Long
    Long sunriseTs = (json?.sys?.sunrise ?: 0L) as Long
    Long sunsetTs  = (json?.sys?.sunset  ?: 0L) as Long

    Integer tzOffsetSec = (json?.timezone ?: 0) as Integer
    ZoneOffset zoneOffset = ZoneOffset.ofTotalSeconds(tzOffsetSec)

    // -------- 4) Conversions & derived --------
    BigDecimal tempC = toC(tempRaw)
    BigDecimal feelC = toC(feelsRaw)
    String windDir   = toCardinal(windDeg)
    String visibilityKm = (visibilityM / 1000.0).toBigDecimal().setScale(1, RoundingMode.HALF_UP).toPlainString()
    String category  = classify(tempC)

    DateTimeFormatter dateFmt     = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("EEE, dd MMM yyyy")
    DateTimeFormatter timeFmt     = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("hh:mm a")
    DateTimeFormatter dateTimeFmt = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("EEE, dd MMM yyyy • hh:mm a")

    String fetchedAtLocal = fmtLocalDateTime(fetchTs, zoneOffset, dateTimeFmt)
    String sunriseLocal   = fmtLocalTime(sunriseTs,  zoneOffset, timeFmt)
    String sunsetLocal    = fmtLocalTime(sunsetTs,   zoneOffset, timeFmt)
    String localDate      = fmtLocalDate(fetchTs,    zoneOffset, dateFmt)

    String tzReadable = zoneOffset.getId()
    if (tzReadable == "Z") tzReadable = "+00:00"

    String color = categoryColor(category)

    // -------- 5) Set properties for Mail / templates --------
    message.setProperty("Weather.City",            city)
    message.setProperty("Weather.Country",         country)
    message.setProperty("Weather.Location",        loc)
    message.setProperty("Weather.Condition",       condition)
    message.setProperty("Weather.Description",     description)
    message.setProperty("Weather.TempC",           "${tempC}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.FeelsLikeC",      "${feelC}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.Humidity",        "${humidity}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.Pressure",        "${pressure}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.VisibilityKm",    visibilityKm)
    message.setProperty("Weather.WindSpeedMS",     "${windSpeed}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.WindDeg",         "${windDeg}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.WindDir",         windDir)
    message.setProperty("Weather.CloudsPct",       "${cloudCover}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.Category",        category)

    message.setProperty("Weather.TimezoneOffset",  tzReadable)
    message.setProperty("Weather.FetchedAtLocal",  fetchedAtLocal)
    message.setProperty("Weather.SunriseLocal",    sunriseLocal)
    message.setProperty("Weather.SunsetLocal",     sunsetLocal)
    message.setProperty("Weather.LocalDate",       localDate)
    message.setProperty("Weather.CategoryColor",   color)
    
    return message
}
  • Click Apply

STEPS 7: Router — Normal vs Default

To evaluate weather severity and decide whether to send an email alert or stop the flow.

  • Drag the Router into the flow.Place it after Groovy

  • Create Two Branches 
    • Branch 1 — Normal Weather (No Email)
      • Click on the first branch line.
      • Add the condition:
      • Expression Type: Non-XML
        • Condition: ${property.Weather.Category} = 'Normal'
        • Connect this branch directly to the End Event. This stops the flow and no email is sent.
    • Branch 2 — Default Branch (Send Email)
      • Click on the second branch.
      • Mark it as Default Route.
      • This path should connect to your End Message → Mail Sender.

This ensures that we only send alerts when the weather crosses defined thresholds.

STEPS 7: Mail Receiver

  • Drag a Receiver to the canvas and connect it to the End Message. 
  • Rename it to Mail. 
    (This name will help you identify that it is used for the weather API call.) 
  • Draw a connector from the End Message step to Mail. 
  • When prompted for adapter selection, choose MAIL Adapter.

Mail Receiver — HTML Email with Modern UI 

  • Connection Tab
    • Address: smtp.gmail.com:587 
    • Authentication: Plain User/Password 
    • Credential Name: Enter the User Credential you created earlier in the Security Material.

  • Processing Tab
    • From: Your Gmail ID (the same email used to generate the App Password) 
    • To: Receiver email address 
    • Subject: 
Weather • ${property.Weather.Location}: ${property.Weather.TempC}°C (${property.Weather.Category}) • ${property.Weather.FetchedAtLocal} UTC${property.Weather.TimezoneOffset} 
    • Body: Paste your HTML + CSS email UI Code inside the Body section. 
    • Body Mime Type: Text/HTML

HTML/CSS Code
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="UTF-8">
<title>Weather Update – ${property.Weather.Location}</title>
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"/>
<style>
  body { font-family: -apple-system, Segoe UI, Roboto, Arial, sans-serif; margin:0; padding:0; color:#1f2937; }
  .container { max-width: 640px; margin: 24px auto; background: #ffffff; border-radius: 16px; overflow: hidden; box-shadow: 0 10px 25px rgba(17,24,39,.08); }
  .header { background: #2563eb; color:#fff; padding: 20px 24px; }
  .title { margin:0; font-size: 20px; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: .2px; }
  .subtitle { margin:6px 0 0; font-size: 13px; opacity: .95; }
  .content { padding: 20px 24px; }
  .tempCard { display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:space-between; background:#f8fafc; border:1px solid #e5e7eb; border-radius:12px; padding:16px; margin:8px; }
  .tempMain { font-size: 38px; font-weight: 800; color:#111827; }
  .tempFeel { font-size: 14px; color:#6b7280; }
  .badge { display:inline-block; padding:6px 12px; font-size:12px; border-radius:999px; background:${property.Weather.CategoryColor}; color:#fff; }
  .row { display:flex; gap:20px; flex-wrap: wrap; }
  .card { flex:1 1 180px; min-width:180px; background:#ffffff; border:1px solid #e5e7eb; border-radius:12px; padding:14px; margin:8px;}
  .card h4 { margin:0 0 8px; font-size:13px; color:#374151; text-transform:uppercase; letter-spacing:.5px; }
  .value { font-size:16px; color:#111827; font-weight:600; }
  .dim { color:#6b7280; font-size:12px; }
  .footer { padding: 16px 24px; background:#f8fafc; border-top:1px solid #e5e7eb; color:#6b7280; font-size:12px; }
</style>
</head>

<body>
  <div class="container">
    
    <div class="header">
      <h1 class="title">Weather in ${property.Weather.Location}</h1>
      <p class="subtitle">
        As of ${property.Weather.FetchedAtLocal} (UTC${property.Weather.TimezoneOffset})
      </p>
    </div>

    <div class="content">
      
      <div class="tempCard">
        <div>
          <div class="tempMain">${property.Weather.TempC}°C</div>
          <div class="tempFeel">
            Feels like ${property.Weather.FeelsLikeC}°C • 
            ${property.Weather.Condition} – ${property.Weather.Description}
          </div>
        </div>
        <div>
          <span class="badge">${property.Weather.Category}</span>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="row">

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Humidity</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.Humidity}%</div>
          <div class="dim">Comfort varies with temp & wind</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Pressure</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.Pressure} hPa</div>
          <div class="dim">Sea-level equivalent</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Visibility</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.VisibilityKm} km</div>
          <div class="dim">Reported by station</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Wind</h4>
          <div class="value">
            ${property.Weather.WindSpeedMS} m/s • 
            ${property.Weather.WindDir} (${property.Weather.WindDeg}°)
          </div>
          <div class="dim">Direction & speed</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Cloud Cover</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.CloudsPct}%</div>
          <div class="dim">Fraction of sky covered</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Sunrise / Sunset</h4>
          <div class="value">
            ${property.Weather.SunriseLocal} • ${property.Weather.SunsetLocal}
          </div>
          <div class="dim">Local times</div>
        </div>

      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="footer">
      Data source: OpenWeather • 
      Local date: ${property.Weather.LocalDate} • 
      Timezone: UTC${property.Weather.TimezoneOffset}
    </div>

  </div>
</body>
</html>

DEPLOY & TESTING

STEPS 1: Deploy the iFlow

  • Click Deploy in CPI. 
  • Wait until the deployment status becomes “Started”. 
  • Go to Monitor → Manage Integration Content. 
  • Confirm the iFlow is Started (green status).

STEPS 2: Test the iFlow 

  • Set the schedule to a short interval (e.g., every 5 minutes) during testing. 
  • Once the scheduled time arrives, CPI will trigger the iFlow automatically.
  • Go to Monitor → Message Processing and verify: 
  • Status: Completed 
  • Check your email inbox for the weather alert.

CONCLUSION

With just a few essential steps—Timer, API call via Request‑Reply, Groovy-based temperature evaluation, and the Mail adapter—you can deliver clear and modern HTML weather alerts exactly when they are needed. This approach can also be adapted for other rule‑based notification scenarios such as air‑quality updates, inventory thresholds, or SLA exception alerts.

If you’d like to extend this further, you can experiment with features like multi‑city summaries, analytical dashboards, or sending notifications to channels like Microsoft Teams or Slack.

If you have any questions or would like to share your feedback, feel free to drop a comment!

entry.title: Efficiency of Remote Service Connections: Redundant requests for connection details and user assignm
entry.content:
Hello,

I am looking for clarification or best practices regarding the remote connection workflow within the SAP Support Portal.

Our internal security policy dictates that remote access is restricted; we strictly open connections and assign specific users to a case only when active troubleshooting is required. Recently, we have observed a recurring pattern where, despite the connection being open and a valid user (with necessary authorizations) being assigned to the incident, SAP Support engineers repeatedly ask for connection details or confirmation of the user assignment.

This leads to unnecessary administrative overhead. We frequently find ourselves "updating" the case simply to restate that the connection is active and the user is already assigned, after which the engineer connects without further issues.

  1. Is there a known synchronization delay between the Service Marketplace/ONE Support Launchpad and the engineers' internal tools?

  2. Are there specific logs or status fields (e.g., in the SAProuter or Service Connector) we should verify to ensure the "Open" status is correctly broadcasted to SAP?

  3. Is this a procedural shift in how SAP Support handles RISE with SAP vs. On-Premise connectivity?

  4. Is this some behavior to postpone processing and get some time? Did you notice it?

Any insights on how to minimize these redundant communication loops would be appreciated.

Thank you

entry.title: Architecting Electronic Bill of Lading on SAP BTP - Rethinking Paper-Based Trade (Part 1 of 3)
entry.content:
Introduction

After participating in the SAP Multidimensional Architect Program at the SAP Academy  in California during Fall 2025, I was particularly inspired by a session titled “From Blueprint to Real Architecture,” which showed how to design end-to-end solutions, one of the cases presented, was international trade scenario, area of my personal interest over the last few years. That experience motivated me to write this Proof of Concept.

This article focuses on the scenario of Ocean Carriers transitioning from paper-based processes to a fully digital platform, aiming to reduce CO₂ emissions while ensuring security and transparency among stakeholders. The solution implements the Electronic Bill of Lading using IMDA's  TradeTrust , an open-source framework that enables the creation of digital trade documents represented as NFT(ERC-721 tokens) on a blockchain network.

This PoC demonstrates how this capability can be deployed on SAP Business Technology Platform, aligned with Digital Container Shipping Association (DCSA)  standards.

 

Article Summary

This three-part series demonstrates how international trade can move from paper-based operations to secure and automate processes while helping organizations achieve their sustainability goals

  • Part 1 – Case Study: Introduces the shift in global trade from paper-based documents to Electronic Bills of Lading.
  • Part 2 – Blueprint:  Describes the key building blocks of the solution, including: event-driven architecture, SAP BTP, Automation, TradeTrust Framework, UI, and the architecture diagram.
  • Part 3 – End-to-End Scenario: Shows the lifecycle of the Electronic Bill of Lading through a real-world scenario.

 

1. Case Study

The global shipping industry, responsible for approximately 90% of world trade, faces pressure to reduce its environmental footprint. Traditional paper-based Bills of Lading, critical for international trade, contribute to deforestation, water pollution, and greenhouse gas emissions through their production, transportation, and disposal.

2. Business Context

Paper Bills of Lading(BL) plays a crucial role in international trade, serve as Receipt, Contract and Document of title. This document is been the core of international trade for centuries.

  • Receipt: The BL, which is issued by the carrier, serves as proof that the cargo has been loaded onto the vessel, documenting the quantity and condition of the goods at the time of shipment.
  • Contract: It acts as a contract of carriage between the shipper, carrier and consignee, defining the agreed terms and conditions of transportation.
  • Document of title: It acts as a document of title, representing ownership of the goods while they are in transit. Possession of the original bill of lading allows the holder to claim the goods upon their arrival or arrange transfer ownership of the cargo to another party in the supply chain.

 

The process typically starts with the carrier issuing the BL to the shipper/seller upon receiving the goods. The shipper/seller then sends the original bill of lading to the buyer, often through a bank, to ensure that the seller receives the payment before the goods are released to the buyer. Finally, the consignee/buyer presents the original bill of lading to the carrier at the destination port to take possession of the goods.

Problem

Traditionally, this has been paper-based, the document must travel physically between banks and buyers across countries, requiring couriers, manual signatures, and physical handovers. Each party checks it manually, which takes 5-10 days, and if the cargo is traded while the ship is at sea, the bill changes hands multiple times. This often causes ships to wait at port, creates demurrage costs, and forces parties to use risky workarounds like letters of indemnity, which carry legal and financial risks.

                                                                 Total time needed for B/L transfer via express courier service: 5-10 days

Solution

The emergence of blockchain technology has enabled the development of Electronic Bills of Lading(eBL), offering not only enhanced security and efficiency but also the potential for a substantial reduction in paper consumption and associated carbon emissions.

Blockchain provides a decentralized and transparent system to manage eBL. The data is not controlled by a single party, but shared across a network where all participants can verify the transactions. Every action, such as issuing or transferring the eBL, is recorded on the blockchain and can be traced by authorized parties. Transactions are validated almost in real time, allowing the eBL to be transferred within minutes instead of several days required with paper documents sent by courier.

By eliminating millions of paper documents and the need for courier shipments, organizations can reduce delays, lower operational costs, minimize the risk of errors, and decrease carbon footprints while aligning with ESG commitments. Electronic bills of lading on an Ethereum Layer 2 can cut carbon emissions by ~96% compared to traditional paper B/Ls.

Electronic Bill of Lading Lifecycle[4]

 

The benefits of the eBL are many and huge. In the announcement, DCSA wrote: “Switching away from the transfer of physical paper bills of lading could save $6.5 billion in direct costs for stakeholders, enable $30-40 billion in annual global trade growth, transform the customer experience and improve sustainability.”

Payment Settlement in International Trade

In traditional international trade, payments are typically secured through banking instruments such as Letters of Credit, documentary collections, or bank guarantees. In these models, banks act as trusted intermediaries that verify trade documents, including the Bill of Lading, before releasing payment from the importer to the exporter. While these mechanisms provide security, they are often manual, time-consuming, and highly dependent on paper-based documentation.

As trade processes move toward digital documentation, new opportunities emerge to integrate payment mechanisms directly with Electronic Bills of Lading.

By combining digital payments with eBL, parts of the trade process could be automated. For example, a smart contract on a blockchain funded by the importer could hold the payment until predefined conditions are met. Once ownership of the eBL is transferred to the importer, the smart contract could automatically release the funds to the exporter. In this context, stablecoins may play an important role by enabling fast and programmable digital settlement.

At the same time, modern banks increasingly provide APIs for Letter of Credit verification and trade finance services. Through networks such as SWIFT, financial institutions can exchange standardized payment messages and verification data. These capabilities can be integrated into digital trade platforms, helping automate document verification.

Although payment is not explored in detail within the scope of this Proof of Concept, the architecture presented here is designed to support such capabilities in the future, allowing payment orchestration to be incorporated as the trade ecosystem continues to evolve.

The Future of Global Trade

In February 2023, DCSA announced a multi-carrier agreement to get to 100% usage of electronic Bills of Lading by 2030.

Thomas Bagge, chief executive officer, DCSA stated, “The digitalization of international trade holds vast potential for the world economy by reducing friction and, as trade brings prosperity and the eBL will further enable trade, helping bring millions out of poverty. Document digitalization has the power to transform and requires collaboration from all stakeholders.”

3. Solution Implementation

In order to develop a fully digitalized solution for international trade that leverages blockchain technology for the Electronic Bill of Lading, the solution should be aligned with industry standards to ensure interoperability, operational efficiency, and regulatory compliance. The solution follows the guidelines established by the Digital Container Shipping Association (DCSA)  and the United Nations Centre for Trade Facilitation and Electronic Business(UN/CEFACT) .

To support the implementation of the Electronic Bill of Lading, this Proof of Concept leverages TradeTrust , an open-source framework developed by the Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA) . TradeTrust provides a neutral infrastructure for the digitization and secure exchange of trade documents, aiming to make international trade safer, faster, easier, and more cost-effective.

The framework uses a public blockchain to maintain a single source-of-truth record of document ownership and aligns with the principles of the MLETR(Model Law on Electronic Transferable Records) , established by the UNCITRAL(United Nations Commission on International Trade Law). 

How TradeTrust Works

Conclusion

In Part 1, I explored the benefits of the Electronic Bill of Lading and its potential to transform the industry—driving sustainability, improving transparency across the supply chain, and accelerating global trade operations.

🔗In Part 2 , I’ll walk through the technical design and core building blocks behind the solution.

entry.title: Re: Receipt from Production: Unit Price Not Calculated fr...
entry.content:
Yes, this is standard behavior, however there is light at the end of the tunnel. Here is how SAP B1 handles product values:

1. When you create production order, you define the quantity of product that you plan to produce, and the quantity of components/resources you plan to use.

2. Based on this data from the order itself, the system calculated planned value of your final product. It does this by calculating the current value of all the components you are planning to use, and divides it by planned quantity of your product. Again, this is planned value.

3. When you issue components, they are issued by their real cost, depending on the selected valuation method.

4. When you receive your product from production, it is received by planned value. It does not matter at this moment what is the exact value of issued components (this is not neccessarily a bad thing, consider an option that maybe you didn't issue any components yet so the product needs to have some value).

5. Now, the situation you described often happend for multiple reasons, there is variance because issued value is different from received value. This is where tha last step of production process kicks in - closing the production order. When you close the production order, the system checks for variance amount, and then revaluates the final product for the variance amount (provided that the final product is still in warehouse). 

So the answer to your question 3 is - make sure that production orders are closed regularly and before the product is issued from the warehouse, so that all variance in production can be removed.

entry.title: Re: How to manage documents in SAP Document AI
entry.content:
Hi Mainak, 
1. We have a retention period feature that you can configure for your tenant. As soon as the documents reach this limit, it will be automatically deleted. If you use our REST APIs, the default retention period is set to 7 days. If you use our OData APIs, then the default is indefinite and you need to set a value in case you want to have them automatically deleted. 
Alternatively, you can manually delete them via UI or API call.

2. We always provide a default client, so you do not need to create one. Clients are only needed if you want to structure your data into different clients, e.g. based on your Line of Business. 

Best, Tobias

entry.title: Read the Right Docs: sIoT vs. eIoT in SAP Asset Performance Management Documentation
entry.content:
The SAP Help Portal is the central resource for understanding and implementing SAP Asset Performance Management (APM). As SAP sunsets legacy IoT-based solutions (sIoT) and introduces Embedded IoT (eIoT), the documentation ensures customers have clear, structured guidance for both environments.

Why Reading the Right Version Matters

Accuracy depends on one simple but critical step: making sure you’re reading the correct version of the documentation. Outdated or unsupported versions can lead to confusion and even errors in implementation.

Question for you: How confident are you that the documentation you’re using today is the correct version?

 

Dual Outputs in the SAP Help Portal

Each deliverable in the portal is published in two outputs:

  • sIoT Output → For customers still using legacy IoT-based APM.
  • eIoT Output → For customers adopting Embedded IoT within SAP S/4HANA.

This dual-output approach allows organizations to continue operating legacy systems while preparing for migration to eIoT. Over time, however, only eIoT documentation will remain available, reflecting SAP’s strategic direction.

Question for you: Do you know which output — sIoT or eIoT — applies to your current setup?

 

Legacy vs. Successor Products

  • Legacy products — SAP IoT, SAP PDMS (Predictive Maintenance and Service), SAP ASPM (Asset Strategy and Performance Management), SAP PAI (Predictive Asset Insights), and SAP APM based on SAP IoT — were sunset in 2022, 2023, and 2024. New customers can no longer purchase these products.
  • The successor to PDMS, PAI, ASPM, and APM based on SAP IoT is APM based on Embedded IoT (APM eIoT).
  • APM eIoT became generally available in October 2024, with all customers and partners requested to complete migration by the end of 2026. By late 2025, 50% of customers had already begun the migration process.
  • Migration requires licensing to the new APM eIoT license. For details, see SAP Note 3598479
Question for you: Has your organization already planned its migration path to APM eIoT?

 

Step-by-Step Navigation

  • Start at the SAP APM Home Page This page introduces Asset Performance Management deliverables and their scope.
  • Identify Deliverable Outputs Each guide, configuration step, and application help is split into two versions:
    • sIoT Output → Legacy IoT-based APM.
    • eIoT Output → Embedded IoT in SAP S/4HANA. Confirm which output applies to your current setup before proceeding.
  • Check the “What’s New” Section
    • Highlights updates, including the sunset timeline for sIoT and the introduction of eIoT features.
    • Use this to track whether a deliverable is still supported or has transitioned to eIoT.
  • Read the Right Version
    • Always confirm you’re aligned with the latest updates.
    • Bookmark the correct documentation site to avoid landing on outdated links.
Question for you: When you navigate the Help Portal, do you actively check the “What’s New” section before diving into a deliverable?

 

Key Takeaway

The SAP Help Portal mirrors the dual-output structure of APM deliverables. The priority is simple: always confirm you’re reading the correct version of our documentation. This ensures accuracy today and prepares you for the future as sIoT is retired and eIoT becomes the standard.

Final Question for you: What steps will you take today to ensure your team is always reading the right version?

 

See also:

  • Blog: Get Help Where You Need it: SAP Companion in SAP APM
  • New video: Access SAP Companion in SAP APM

entry.title: Re: How to delete all data from SAP Asset Performance Man...
entry.content:
To delete all the data from APM you will need to unsubscribe and subscribe APM application.

Please note

If you unsubscribe the solution, all data related to the solution will be irrevocably deleted. Unsubscribing and re-subscribing SAP Asset Performance Management will create a new subscription.

To unsubscribe the relevant solution from your subaccount, follow the preceding steps:

  1. Choose Services   Instances and Subscriptions in the side menu.
  2. Go the relevant subscription.
  3. Choose Actions  Delete.

Subscribing to SAP Asset Performance Management

entry.title: Need a workflow to review and approve the sales return order.
entry.content:
The requirement is that the client needs a workflow to review and approve sales return orders in the SAP Public Cloud System.

Detailed Step-by-Step:

  1. The client requires a workflow to be implemented in the system.
  2. This workflow should handle sales return orders.
  3. The workflow must include a review step, where the relevant personnel can examine the sales return order.
  4. After the review, there should be an approval step to authorize the sales return order.
  5. The entire process should be configured and executed within the SAP Public Cloud system.

entry.title: New ABAP program no analyze payroll performance
entry.content:
Hi HCM Payroll Community,

If you are running your payroll in background, including Payroll Control Center and Process Model (PEST & PUST), this blog may be interesting for you.

In just a few minutes, you can create a program that automatically analyzes how good or bad your payroll performance is:

Execute and here's the result:

- Scroll to the end

The most important piece of information here is the Perf. which measures the speed in seconds / period, so the lower the better. 0,20 means 5 periods / second and 1,00 means 1 period / second. 

Note that we do not measure seconds / employee because one employee with 12 months (periods) of retro run would take similar as 12 employees with no retro.

Once you identify that you really have performance issues with your payroll and it's not something else, next step is to conduct an ABAP trace using trx SAT or ST12.

Further information can be found in kba https://me.sap.com/notes/3732217

entry.title: How to enable an automatic email of an Invoice, IRN and E-waybill PDF's
entry.content:
How to enable an automatic emailing of invoice and IRN/E-waybill PDFs to multiple recipients (e.g., Dispatch In-charge, ASM and Stores).

Step-by-Step Requirement Description

  1. Document Generation
    • The system should generate the following documents upon billing:
      • Customer Invoice (PDF)
      • IRN (Invoice Reference Number) document
      • E-Way Bill document (PDF)
  2. Automatic Email Trigger
    • Once the billing document is successfully created, an automatic email should be triggered by the system without manual intervention.
  3. Multiple Recipients Handling
    • The email should be sent to multiple recipients, including:
      • Dispatch In-charge
      • Area Sales Manager (ASM)
      • Stores Department

entry.title: Mandatory Billing from Primary Storage Locations Across All Plants
entry.content:
How to Enforce Mandatory Billing from Primary Storage Locations Across All Plants

Step 1:
The client requires billing to be done only from the main storage location in each plant.

Step 2:
The organization operates 27 plants, and this rule applies to all of them.

Step 3:
Billing must not be done from any other storage location.

Step 4:
The client requires that all billing activities be carried out exclusively from the designated main storage location within each plant.

Final Step 5:
The client requires that all billing activities be carried out exclusively from the designated main storage location within each plant. The organization operates 27 plants, and for each plant, billing must strictly originate from its respective primary storage location. Under no circumstances should billing be processed from any alternate storage location.

entry.title: Re: Update Schedule Line Data using SD_SALESDOCUMENT_CHANGE
entry.content: This is a classic nuance with SD_SALESDOCUMENT_CHANGE: the delivery date field (VBEP-EDATU) is directly updatable, but the subsequent dates (GI date, loading date, material availability date, transportation planning date) are derived by SAP’s scheduling logic, not stored independently. That is why they update correctly in SE37 when you pass only schedule line data (the FM triggers full scheduling), but not in your Fiori app call where you also pass header/item structures. In your Fiori app, you need to explicitly trigger scheduling (via LOGIC_SWITCH) or use BAPI_SALESORDER_CHANGE. That will ensure all dates update consistently, just like in your SE37 test.

entry.title: Re: Possibility to change status of purchasing documents
entry.content:
Hi,

In the app: My Purchase Requisitions (F1639A)

The status “Approved” means:

  • The PR has passed workflow/release strategy
  • It is technically fully released

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, standard document statuses such as ‘Approved’ cannot be renamed. These statuses are system-defined. To reflect your business terminology like - submitted, we recommend submitting an idea through the SAP Customer Influence Portal, which is reviewed by Product Managers for potential inclusion in future releases. You can follow the steps in this KBA to submit your idea 

  1. Get started by opening SAP Customer Influence SAP Continuous Influence sessions for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition 
  2. Register and accept the Terms of Use
  3. Choose a session of your interest
  4. Create and submit your improvement request
  5. Take a look at existing improvement requests and start voting and commenting

Thanks and regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: 5HP+MTO how to realize the 5HP+MTO function
entry.content:
Good day @jesseho271 

Thank you for your question.

The setup you described mixes two different process patterns, and that is why your test is not producing your expected sales order stock/E stock demand.

I would not recommend approaching this as “5HP directly creates MTO demand for E stock from an advanced intercompany purchase order”. I would approach it this way instead:

  1. 5HP is the scope item for Advanced Intercompany Stock Transfer.
  2. 5W2 is Group Valuation, which is relevant for valuation and for handling intercompany profit, but it is not the scope item that activates MTO planning logic.
  3. The MTO in Public Cloud is tied to sales order based planning strategies, especially strategy 20, where the sales order creates an individual customer requirement and MRP creates planned orders for that requirement.

So, if your current test starts from an NIBC and you expect MRP to automatically create E stock demand, that is the wrong starting point for standard MTO in Public Cloud. Sales order requirement is responsible for triggering MTO, not the intercompany PO by itself.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/3b24bf53d25ab64ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/40d700f2876d4de8832c0050f8fd7940.html?locale=en-US

One can therefore say that the issue you face is like not that MRP is malfunctioning. The issue is that the process document you are using for the test does not create the sales order specific planning requirement needed for special stock E in the Public Cloud MTO.

For your specific requirement, a scope that is much closer to it is the Advance Intercompany Sales. MTO and Variant Config (7DM) is a related intercompany sale plus MTO scenario. This shows that SAP treats advanced intercompany plus MTO as a dedicated process combination on the sales side, not as a simple extension of 5HP stock transfer logic.

So, if your target business requirement is external customer order, intercompany fulfillment across company codes, MTO planning, special stock/sales order linked production, then the process to evaluate is likely advanced intercompany sales with the MTO related scope, rather than testing only 5HP advanced intercompany stock transfer from an NIBC purchase order.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/db81ae38a02d46fcbe6517ee266ede62.html?locale=en-US

3651704 - Configuration of scope item 7DM - SAP for Me

3662407 - intercompany sales order item category CBIC does not trigger production orders in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

My recommended approach is as follows:

  • If your goal is true MTO with special stock E, is to use the customer sales order to trigger the process and verify that the material and plant are set up with strategy 20 so that the sales order creates an individual requirement. Then you can run MRP and check whether the planned order carries the sales order assignment.
  • If your goal is advanced intercompany fulfillment from one company to another, you can use 5HP for the stock transfer and intercompany valuation flow, but don’t expect the advanced intercompany PO alone to behave like a customer order based MTO trigger for E stock.
  • If you want to integrate advanced intercompany plus MTO in one scenario, then review whether your intended business process should instead be tested with the advanced intercompany sales MTO related scope reference through 7DM.

 

 Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Send Email with inline BASE64 Image in SAP ABAP.
entry.content:
Introduction 

I recently came across the need to send an automatically generated email based on a given HTML template. To my surprise, this time the template arrived with embedded/inline (src="data:image/png;base64,...) images using Base64 encoding. I should mention that all previous HTML mail templates I worked with contained image links pointing to a content web server.

Since this was new to me, I did some research on the topic but did not find much information, except for this helpful blog post:
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/send-email-with-image-from-mime-repository-in-sap-abap/ba-p/14285931 

In that scenario, the images are stored in the MIME repository, whereas in my case they are embedded directly in the HTML file. Therefore, I needed to extract the picture content from the HTML code, rebuild the template, and generate an email using standard SAP BCS functionality.

After implementing this successfully, I would like to share some of my coding approach with the community.

Code Example: 

REPORT zemail_to_send_img2.

*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Data Declarations
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
TYPES: BEGIN OF lty_pic_str,
         name TYPE string,
         mime TYPE w3conttype,
         src  TYPE xstring,
       END OF lty_pic_str.

DATA: lv_html_in  TYPE string,               "HTML body string (input)
      lv_html_out TYPE string,               "HTML body string (output)
      lt_html_out TYPE soli_tab,             "HTML body lines
      lv_pic_cnt  TYPE i,                    "Picture counter
      lv_src      TYPE xstring,              "Image content in XSTRING
      lt_solix    TYPE solix_tab,            "Binary table
      lv_len      TYPE so_obj_len,           "Length of binary content
      lt_pic_tab  TYPE TABLE OF lty_pic_str. "Table with pictures

DATA: lo_mime_srv  TYPE REF TO cl_gbt_multirelated_service,
      lo_doc       TYPE REF TO cl_document_bcs,
      lo_bcs       TYPE REF TO cl_bcs,
      lo_sender    TYPE REF TO if_sender_bcs,
      lo_recipient TYPE REF TO if_recipient_bcs.

*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 1: Build HTML Email Body
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
lv_html_in =
   |<html>|
&& |<!-- To work with BCS, styles should be defined locally !!! -->|
&& |<body style="background-color:#e6f0fa; font-family:Arial; margin:0; padding:10px;">|
&& |<div style="max-width:600px; margin:auto; background:#ffffff; padding:20px; border-radius:8px; border:1px solid #d0d7e3;">|
&& | <div style="text-align:center; margin-bottom:15px;">|
&& |<!-- Inline BASE64 PNG Image -->|
&& | <img src="data:image/png;base64,|
&& |iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAAKMAAACUCAMAAADIzWmnAAAAY1BMVEX///8AAABHR0f7+/vz8/PV1dXw8PA6Ojr29vbk5OTp6ekyMj|
&& |Ls7OwpKSnc3NxAQECcnJyRkZFfX1/MzMxkZGStra2+vr5WVlZQUFB1dXUeHh6mpqaLi4sPDw99fX1vb28XFxcA3zptAAAMl0lEQVR4|
&& |nO1c52LyvA6G7EH2hCzu/yo/Sw7BQ06B0vGe0+dPm0Esy1qWZR8Of/jDH/7wh/97RG7bDnkxAop8aCs3+mmSRERtUVp+cpmOd0yXxL|
&& |fqovoFhAZpO/vHPfhjmwY/SGGaL7FGVKKTuQzhzxDoNOVFpOSCV6UbuOX9cnu4tN/PTLeQaDiWOZK24MMFic076Y04d7+Xwlke0a71|
&& |DhX723MViXr2f3XwWoXKOf0+Csu1zZJLYwkMOjHVuVTrGxVjsn+Cf/irVr3K7fI9VKbzlbc7tAtqRIOSNrB/x+2lkV0N8I/TAHXXuR|
&& |osLpjj16uPPXBT4zdehDwqeZseXHjbax484ZcpDvjZ8Rr+y6z5Ygpd3k7Zsrbhn+utwZZdFMKbBbtu1/8HFAw29C0f+f5LB3zEYU4a|
&& |hwllBg3fyDiw1hNRb12mVOXtogEN60IYedS1ZPgyCl0uUgWMoQti5m9URTCctvCufWZ3NkeIb1tw6RVHQULeDs6DGnU3hDHP7g0Nwt|

&& |alqU/r5FBR0kRhY3WxhwypZ2PWNoGr/lJCS7Wo+J8js+8ngAE5zVtEkkSCOe1bQe6xSX65ym/r5zr6qzPK9eKgclUjzzqtXOvErm7z|
&& |2ZqxrlOWw96GeHlTKFxfdSCHCaTppA75/BVv7AGWyINF/0fIV+J17y7zkyjMbvPxNwRdSO9NmKbLL7C85WvEM9o7L9XWdU/uEPf/jD|
&& |H34G/wGmG7NImfqvDAAAAABJRU5ErkJggg==|
&& |" alt="World" style="width:80px;" />|
&& | </div>|
&& | <p style="font-size:12px; color:#000;text-align:center">|
&& |   <b>HELLO WORLD!!!</b><br><br>|
&& | </p>|
&& | </div>|
&& |</body></html>|.


*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 2: Extract all pictures and build new HTML Email Body
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
lv_html_out = lv_html_in.

" Get All Images...
FIND ALL OCCURRENCES OF REGEX `<img\b[^>]*>` IN lv_html_in RESULTS DATA(lt_matches) IGNORING CASE.
LOOP AT lt_matches ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<m>).
  DATA(lv_img) = lv_html_in+<m>-offset(<m>-length).

  " Get Source...
  FIND FIRST OCCURRENCE OF REGEX `src="([^"]+)"` IN lv_img SUBMATCHES DATA(lv_source) IGNORING CASE.
  IF sy-subrc = 0 AND lv_source IS NOT INITIAL.

    " BASE64...
    IF to_lower( lv_source ) CS ';base64,'.

      " Get Picture...
      FIND FIRST OCCURRENCE OF REGEX ';base64,(.*)$' IN lv_source SUBMATCHES DATA(lv_base64) IGNORING CASE.
      IF sy-subrc = 0 AND lv_base64 IS NOT INITIAL.

        ADD 1 TO lv_pic_cnt.

        " Get Picture Name...
        DATA(lv_pic_name) = |inline_image_{ lv_pic_cnt }.png|.

        " Append Image Table...
        CLEAR: lv_src.
        CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_BASE64_DECODE_STR'
          EXPORTING
            input  = lv_base64
          IMPORTING
            output = lv_src
          EXCEPTIONS
            failed = 1
            OTHERS = 2.
        IF sy-subrc <> 0.
          CONTINUE.
        ENDIF.
        " Mime type is hardcoded here for simplicity...
        APPEND VALUE #( name = lv_pic_name
                        mime = 'image/png'
                        src=lv_src  ) TO lt_pic_tab[].

        " Build new Source...
        DATA(lv_new_source) = |cid:{ lv_pic_name }|.

        " Replace Source...
        DATA(lv_new_img) = lv_img.
        REPLACE FIRST OCCURRENCE OF lv_source IN lv_new_img WITH lv_new_source.

        " Replace Image...
        REPLACE FIRST OCCURRENCE OF lv_img IN lv_html_out WITH lv_new_img.
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.


*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 3: Convert HTML-Out STRING to SOLIX
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
lt_html_out[] = cl_document_bcs=>string_to_soli( lv_html_out ).


*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 4: Create MIME Multi-Part Object for Email
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE OBJECT lo_mime_srv.


*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 5: Add Inline Images (Content-ID Based Embedding)
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
LOOP AT lt_pic_tab ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_pic>).
  CLEAR: lt_solix[], lv_len.
  lt_solix = cl_bcs_convert=>xstring_to_solix( <fs_pic>-src ).
  lv_len   = xstrlen( <fs_pic>-src ).

  lo_mime_srv->add_binary_part(
    content      = lt_solix
    content_type = <fs_pic>-mime
    length       = lv_len
    content_id   = <fs_pic>-name ).
ENDLOOP.


*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 6: Set HTML Body into MIME Service
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
lo_mime_srv->set_main_html(
  content     = lt_html_out
  filename    = 'email.htm'
  description = 'Hello World!' ).

*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 7: Create Email Document with Subject
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
TRY.
    lo_doc = cl_document_bcs=>create_from_multirelated(
      i_subject          = 'Hello World!'
      i_multirel_service = lo_mime_srv ).
  CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lx_root).
    WRITE: /, |Something went wrong....|. RETURN.
ENDTRY.

*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Step 8: Create and Send Email Using BCS
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
TRY.
    lo_bcs = cl_bcs=>create_persistent( ).
    lo_bcs->set_document( lo_doc ).

    lo_sender = cl_sapuser_bcs=>create( sy-uname ).
    lo_bcs->set_sender( lo_sender ).

    lo_recipient = cl_cam_address_bcs=>create_internet_address(
                     'hello.world@gmail.com' ).
    lo_bcs->add_recipient( lo_recipient ).

    lo_bcs->send( i_with_error_screen = abap_true ).

  CATCH cx_root INTO lx_root.
    WRITE: /, |Something went wrong....|. RETURN.
ENDTRY.

COMMIT WORK AND WAIT.

*----------------------------------------------------------------------
* Final Message
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
WRITE / 'Email sent successfully Check It in SOST. !'.

 Output: 

Explanation

The program performs the following major steps:

  1. Construct an HTML template containing inline Base64‑encoded images. (But you may get it as local file on your PC or form MIME repository. These options are not considered here ).
  2. Scan the HTML for <img> tags and extract the Base64 image data. 
  3. Decode each Base64 image with the help of function module SCMS_BASE64_DECODE_STRE and store it as a binary part for the email. 
  4. Replace Base64 data in HTML with CID references (e.g., cid:inline_image_1.png).
  5. Convert HTML to BCS format (SO_DOCUMENT format).
  6. Create a MIME multi-part message via class CL_GBT_MULTIRELATED_SERVICE.
  7. Embed all images as MIME parts so email clients can render them inline.
  8. Assemble the email document with subject, HTML body, and images.
  9. Send the email using BCS to the configured recipient.

This ABAP report builds an HTML email containing Base64‑encoded inline images, extracts and decodes those images, embeds them as MIME parts in the email, rewrites the HTML to use Content‑ID (CID) references, and sends the fully constructed HTML email with inline images using the SAP BCS framework. The email is sent from the logged-on SAP user to a fixed email address and can be traced via transaction SOST.

 

Thank you for taking the time to read my post. I hope this information helps anyone facing a similar requirement. If you have questions, feedback, or suggestions for improvement, feel free to leave a comment below.
